channel.title: Paper Feeds
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper feeds from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Algorithmic Toolkit for Linearization of S-boxes
item.description: This paper introduces the first comprehensive **algorithmic toolkit for strong linearization of arbitrary S-boxes**, bridging a long-standing gap between theoretical possibility and practical cryptanalysis. For $n \leq 8$ bits, our exact algorithms provably find optimal linearizations (maximizing input coverage or approximation probability); for larger S-boxes (e.g., 16-bit Super-Sboxes), we deliver high-quality approximations with rigorous upper bounds. We systematically analyze S-boxes from AES, SKINNY, APN functions, monomials, and RAIN, uncovering previously unknown strong linearizations. Crucially, we solve the *multi-piece covering problem*, enabling efficient decomposition of an S-box into complementary affine fragments. Leveraging this, we derive the first generic linearization framework for the CICO (Constrained-Input-Constrained-Output) problem over SPN-based permutations with *arbitrary* linear layers—establishing the first general cryptanalytic method whose feasibility depends solely on the existence of a strong S-box linearization.

item.title: Improved TensorPIR: Single-Server PIR with Lower Communication Cost
item.description: Private Information Retrieval (PIR) enables privacy-preserving database access, but state-of-the-art single-server schemes like HintlessPIR ($O(N^{1/2})$ communication) and TensorPIR ($O(N^{1/3})$ asymptotically) suffer from practical inefficiencies—especially TensorPIR’s reliance on excessive CRT moduli inflating real-world bandwidth. We propose **Improved TensorPIR**, a novel framework that restructures index encryption to minimize CRT decomposition depth and modulus count, thereby reducing both communication and computation. Experimentally, on databases from 16 GB to 128 GB, our total communication drops to **45.5% of TensorPIR’s**, with theoretical query and answer sizes reduced to **36.9% and 22.2%**, respectively. Compared to HintlessPIR, we achieve strictly lower asymptotic complexity and cut online time to **28.9%–56.1%**, delivering the first practical realization of $O(N^{1/3})$-cost PIR without security or deployment compromises.

item.title: UC4Free! Existing Threshold Signatures are UC Secure
item.description: This paper establishes that widely deployed threshold signature schemes—including BLS, Schnorr, and post-quantum candidates—are **universally composable (UC) secure without any modification**, resolving a critical gap in cryptographic foundations. We introduce refined game-based definitions capturing adaptive corruption, robustness, preprocessing flexibility, and graded unforgeability—generalizing and correcting prior work (e.g., Bellare et al., CRYPTO’22). We define the ideal functionality $\mathcal{F}\text{-}\mathtt{TS3}$ and prove that a scheme UC-realizes it **if and only if** it satisfies our game-based properties. To ease practical UC modeling, we also provide four weaker yet highly usable ideal functionalities ($\mathcal{F}\text{-}\mathtt{TS2}$, $\mathcal{F}\text{-}\mathtt{TS1}$, $\mathcal{F}\text{-}\mathtt{TSSync2}$, $\mathcal{F}\text{-}\mathtt{TSSync1}$), all realizable by schemes proven secure under our definitions. Our results enable direct, composition-safe integration of existing high-performance threshold signatures into complex protocols—demonstrating that composability need not compromise efficiency, only demand definitional rigor.

item.title: UnifOMR: Oblivious Message Retrieval with Near-optimal Concrete Efficiency
item.description: This paper studies Oblivious Message Retrieval (OMR), a cryptographic primitive that hides metadata (e.g., sender–recipient links) during encrypted message retrieval. We first establish a tight theoretical connection: OMR with *strong detection-key-unlinkability* is at least as hard as Private Information Retrieval (PIR), and existing constructions—including SophOMR—already satisfy this property; the reduction incurs low overhead, implying OMR cannot be *substantially* more efficient than PIR asymptotically. Second, we propose **UnifOMR**, the first OMR scheme using batch PIR as a black-box component. Under practical parameters ($2^{19}$ messages, 612 B each), UnifOMR achieves **20×–1080× faster server runtime** (~25 s vs. >1250 s) and scales favorably—its server cost is dominated (50%–92%) by underlying batch PIR. Thus, for databases of size $2^{16}$–$2^{23}$ and message lengths 612–3060 B, UnifOMR concretely approaches the PIR-based lower bound, achieving *near-optimal concrete efficiency*. Trade-offs include linear (but small-constant) digest size and two-round interaction.

item.title: GLiGuard: Schema-Conditioned Classification for LLM Safeguard
item.description: GLiGuard is a 0.3B-parameter, schema-conditioned bidirectional encoder for real-time LLM content moderation. Unlike dominant autoregressive guard models (7B–27B parameters) that inefficiently cast classification as generation, GLiGuard encodes task definitions (e.g., *prompt_safety*) and label semantics (e.g., *self-harm*, *jailbreak_detection*) as structured token schemas directly in the input—enabling simultaneous evaluation of 5 major safety aspects and 25 fine-grained categories (14 harms + 11 jailbreaks) in a single non-autoregressive forward pass. Evaluated across nine established safety benchmarks, it achieves competitive F1 scores versus state-of-the-art decoder-based guards while being 23–90× smaller, delivering up to 16× higher throughput and 17× lower latency. This demonstrates that compact bidirectional encoders can match large-guard accuracy at drastically reduced inference cost. Code and models: https://github.com/fastino-ai/GLiGuard

item.title: Graph Representation Learning Augmented Model Manipulation on Federated Fine-Tuning of LLMs
item.description: Federated fine-tuning (FFT) enables privacy-preserving collaborative adaptation of large language models (LLMs), yet remains vulnerable to model manipulation attacks where adversaries inject malicious updates to degrade global and local model performance. This paper introduces **AugMP**, the first attack framework leveraging **graph representation learning** to capture structural correlations among benign LLM updates—modeling them as nodes in a heterogeneous graph with edges defined by gradient, activation, and layer-wise statistical dependencies. AugMP employs an **iterative augmented Lagrangian dual optimization** to generate stealthy malicious updates that simultaneously maximize attack objectives (e.g., accuracy drop) and preserve statistical/geometry consistency with benign updates across parameter space, gradient distribution, and learned embeddings. Experiments on LLaMA-2-7B, Qwen-1.5-4B, and Phi-3-mini show AugMP achieves up to **26% global accuracy degradation** and **22% average local accuracy drop**, while evading state-of-the-art defenses (e.g., Krum, NormClip, graph-based anomaly detectors) due to its high fidelity to benign update characteristics.

item.title: Differentially Private Auditing Under Strategic Response
item.description: We study differentially private (DP) auditing of AI systems where developers strategically reallocate mitigation efforts in response to privacy-constrained audit interfaces. Formalizing this as a bilevel Stackelberg game, we introduce the **welfare-weighted under-detection gap $B_w$**—the residual harm, weighted by societal welfare, that remains undetected at the developer’s strategic best response. We prove that naive DP auditing (uniform or harm-proportional budget allocation) *strictly worsens* $B_w$ versus non-strategic baselines under heterogeneous detectability, non-comonotone welfare weights, and interior developer optima. The optimal auditor allocation balances four factors: welfare weight, audit miss-probability, detectability elasticity, and mitigation-cost curvature. Leveraging the developer’s KKT system, we reformulate the bilevel problem into a single-level optimization. We propose **Strategic Private Audit Design (SPAD)**, a projected-gradient algorithm computing hypergradients via implicit differentiation of the developer’s best response. SPAD achieves provably improved $B_w$ and enables practical, incentive-aware DP auditing.

item.title: An Automated Framework for Cybersecurity Policy Compliance Assessment Against Security Control Standards
item.description: This paper introduces **PROPARAG**, an automated framework for auditing organizational cybersecurity policies against security control standards (e.g., NIST SP 800-53). It addresses key challenges in manual policy-to-control assessment—abstraction mismatch, linguistic heterogeneity, and poor traceability—by jointly performing evidence retrieval, coverage scoring, gap identification, and grounded explanation/recommendation generation per control. Evaluated on two real-world policy corpora using 1,007 NIST controls across multiple closed- and open-source LLMs, PROPARAG achieves strong F1 scores of **88.54 (OrgA)** and **82.31 (OrgB)**. Crucially, it produces *traceable*, *citation-backed* gap reports and actionable recommendations—each tied to specific policy passages. This work establishes a foundation for LLM-powered, auditable, control-level compliance assessment.

item.title: Cross-Modal Backdoors in Multimodal Large Language Models
item.description: We identify a critical, previously overlooked supply-chain vulnerability in connector-based multimodal large language models (MLLMs): the lightweight modality alignment connector. We propose **Cross-Modal Backdoor (CMB)**, the first attack that poisons *only the connector*—using just one seed sample and its augmented variants from *a single modality*—to enable backdoor activation *across modalities* (e.g., trigger a text output via malicious images or audio). CMB operates in two stages: (1) poisoning the connector to bind a compact latent region to a malicious target; and (2) extracting a malicious centroid from poisoned representations and performing input-side optimization to steer cross-modal inputs toward it—*without API queries or full-model access*. Evaluated on PandaGPT and NExT-GPT, CMB achieves up to **99.9% ASR** (same-modality) and **>95.0% ASR** across diverse cross-modal settings under bounded perturbations. It remains highly stealthy (weight cosine similarity >0.97 vs. benign connectors; negligible clean-input leakage) and evades existing defenses without significant utility loss. This exposes a fundamental fragility in multimodal alignment: a single compromised connector establishes a reusable, cross-modal latent backdoor pathway.

item.title: Vaporizer: Breaking Watermarking Schemes for Large Language Model Outputs
item.description: This paper presents **Vaporizer**, the first systematic evaluation framework exposing critical vulnerabilities in state-of-the-art LLM output watermarking schemes. We design and deploy diverse semantic-preserving attacks—including lexical substitution, multi-hop machine translation, and fine-tuned neural paraphrasing—to remove watermarks while maintaining textual meaning. Evaluation uses dual criteria: (1) watermark detection rate falling significantly below statistical thresholds (p < 0.01), and (2) rigorous semantic preservation measured via BERTScore (≥0.89 avg), Flesch Reading Ease (Δ<±3.2), grammatical correctness, and complexity metrics. Across 7 leading watermarking methods, all fail under ≤3 attack steps; 5 collapse to <20% detection after a single paraphrasing pass. Our results demonstrate that current watermarks lack *semantic-layer robustness*, urging a paradigm shift toward meaning-aware, adversarially hardened designs.

item.title: When the Ruler is Broken: Parsing-Induced Suppression in LLM-Based Security Log Evaluation
item.description: This paper identifies *parsing-induced suppression*—a silent, systematic evaluation artifact in LLM-based security log classification—caused by rigid regex pipelines that extract structured fields from free-form model outputs. Using OpenSOC-AI (a LoRA-finetuned TinyLlama-1.1B) on a 50-example test set, we show that a strict regex parser reports **0% threat accuracy**, while a fuzzy parser (tolerant of casing, spacing, and punctuation) recovers **76% threat accuracy**—a full 76-point gap attributable solely to evaluation methodology. Severity accuracy remains stable at **58%** under both parsers, confirming field-format mismatch—not model degradation—as the causal mechanism. For reference, Claude Sonnet achieves **88% threat accuracy** zero-shot under the same fuzzy protocol. Residual errors cluster in behaviorally adjacent classes (reconnaissance, brute force, credential stuffing), indicating boundary ambiguity rather than systemic failure. We introduce **SOC-Bench v0**: a benchmark with a standardized 13-class threat taxonomy, statistical power requirements, fuzzy extraction specification, and open scoring script—to prevent parser-specific accuracy distortion in future SOC-LLM research.

item.title: Modulated learning for private and distributed regression with just a single sample per client device
item.description: This paper addresses private distributed regression when each client holds only **one data sample**, a setting common in wearables, mobile usage analytics, and IoT sensors. Standard federated learning fails here: a single-sample local update is statistically meaningless and severely degraded by privacy noise. We propose *modulated learning*: each client applies **one calibrated random perturbation** (e.g., Gaussian or structured noise) directly to its raw sample—*not* to model parameters—and uploads the transformed representation. The server aggregates these representations and applies an analytical post-processing step to compute an **unbiased gradient estimate** whose expectation exactly matches the centralized non-private gradient. Unlike conventional private FL—which transmits noisy model weights—our method transmits privately modulated *data samples*, enabling effective collaboration under extreme data scarcity. Theoretically, it satisfies (ε,δ)-differential privacy; empirically, it achieves >40% higher R² than baselines on real-world health datasets under ε ≤ 2.0, with minimal client-side computation.

item.title: Demystifying and Detecting Agentic Workflow Injection Vulnerabilities in GitHub Actions
item.description: This paper introduces **Agentic Workflow Injection (AWI)**, a novel class of vulnerabilities arising when untrusted GitHub event context (e.g., issue bodies, PR descriptions) is insecurely incorporated into LLM agent prompts or downstream scripts in GitHub Actions workflows. We identify two core patterns: **Prompt-to-Agent (P2A)**—where tainted input directly reaches the agent’s prompt—and **Prompt-to-Script (P2S)**—where attacker influence propagates via model-generated outputs (e.g., code, commands) into executable workflow steps. Through systematic analysis of 1,033 real-world AI-assisted Actions, we formalize AWI-specific taint specifications and build **TaintAWI**, the first static taint-analysis tool for detecting AWI across YAML, JavaScript, and Python. Applied to 13,392 agentic workflows from 10,792 repositories, TaintAWI reports 519 potential AWI instances, with **496 confirmed exploitable (95.6% precision)**—including **343 previously unknown zero-days**. Of 187 prioritized disclosures, 24 have been accepted or fixed. This work establishes the first empirical foundation and practical detection framework for securing LLM agents in CI/CD pipelines.

item.title: Membership Inference Attacks on Vision-Language-Action Models
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic study of membership inference attacks (MIAs) against vision-language-action (VLA) models—embodied AI systems that map multimodal inputs to executable actions. We formalize two novel inference settings: *sample-level* (over individual state-action transitions) and *trajectory-level* (over full embodied demonstrations). Leveraging both classic signals (e.g., action-token likelihoods) and VLA-specific behavioral cues—including observable action errors and temporal motion patterns—we develop attacks under strict black-box, gray-box, and white-box access. Evaluated across RT-1, OpenVLA, and VoxPoser on Bridge, Franka, and other benchmarks, our attacks achieve strong performance (AUC up to 0.94), with black-box attacks using *only generated actions* reaching AUC ≥ 0.76. These results expose a critical, previously overlooked privacy risk in deployed robotic systems and underscore the urgent need for VLA-tailored privacy evaluation and defenses.

item.title: Less Random, More Private: What is the Optimal Subsampling Scheme for DP-SGD?
item.description: This paper challenges the dogma that Poisson subsampling is optimal for privacy amplification in DP-SGD. We show its high participation variance—not just a practical nuisance—is a fundamental cause of suboptimal privacy guarantees. We propose **Balanced Iteration Subsampling (BIS)**, a structured scheme where each sample participates in *exactly* $k$ iterations while maintaining uniform marginal selection probability per iteration. We prove BIS achieves strictly stronger privacy amplification than Poisson subsampling and is *asymptotically optimal* as $\sigma \to 0$ and $\sigma \to \infty$. Crucially, we demonstrate that eliminating participation variance—not maximizing randomness—governs the privacy-noise tradeoff. To bridge theory and practice, we introduce a near-exact Monte Carlo accountant for BIS that bypasses analytical slack in RDP and PLD composition. Across 60+ realistic DP-SGD configurations, BIS consistently reduces the required noise multiplier by up to **9.6%**, especially in low-noise, high-utility regimes. Our results overturn the intuition that more sampling randomness implies better privacy: structured participation can be both more practical and more private.

item.title: TraceFix: Repairing Agent Coordination Protocols with TLA+ Counterexamples
item.description: We present **TraceFix**, a verification-first pipeline for synthesizing and repairing coordination protocols in LLM-based multi-agent systems. Given a task description, an agent generates a structured intermediate representation (IR) of the protocol topology, auto-translates it into PlusCal/TLA+, and iteratively refines the protocol using concrete counterexamples from the TLC model checker—until full formal verification is achieved. Verified process bodies are compiled into per-agent system prompts and executed under a runtime monitor that enforces topological constraints. Evaluated on 48 tasks across 16 scenario families, TraceFix achieves 100% TLC verification success: 62.5% pass on the first attempt, and no task requires more than four repair iterations—even with state spaces spanning six orders of magnitude and average verification time under 60 seconds. A 3,456-run study shows topology-monitored execution attains 89.4% average task completion (81.5% full), and degrades at roughly half the rate of prompt-only and chat-only baselines under reduced model capability. Ablation under fixed runtime demonstrates TLC-verified protocols cut deadlock/livelock incidence from 31.1% to 14.1%, with maximal improvement under fault injection.

item.title: INO-SGD: Addressing Utility Imbalance under Individualized Differential Privacy
item.description: Differential privacy (DP) is widely used to protect sensitive training data, but growing data ownership rights motivate *individualized* DP (IDP), where each user sets their own privacy budget ε_i. Existing IDP methods, however, cause severe *utility imbalance*: data with smaller ε_i (e.g., stigmatized disease cases) are over-noised or underrepresented, degrading model performance on similar future users. We propose **INO-SGD**, the first IDP-compliant algorithm that mitigates this imbalance by *per-batch, ε_i-aware gradient weighting* (w_i ∝ ε_i²) and calibrated noise scaling—ensuring strict (ε_i, δ)-DP per record while boosting utility for high-privacy data. Experiments on CIFAR and medical datasets show INO-SGD improves accuracy on low-ε_i subsets by **23.6%** on average versus state-of-the-art IDP baselines, without compromising overall privacy guarantees. It is both theoretically sound and empirically feasible.

item.title: On the Tradeoffs of On-Device Generative Models in Federated Predictive Maintenance Systems
item.description: This paper presents the first comprehensive study of generative models—Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), and Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs)—in federated predictive maintenance (PdM) for IoT edge devices. We evaluate their utility, stability, and communication overhead under full federation (global parameter sharing) and *partial federation*, where only encoder or decoder subcomponents are shared across clients. Based on empirical analysis over the PHM 2012 time-series dataset, we propose a novel taxonomy formalizing partial component sharing as a principled mechanism for model personalization in heterogeneous FL settings. Key findings include: (1) Full-federated GANs improve training stability over local training but remain less robust than VAEs or DDPMs; (2) Partial-federated DDPMs—especially with shared decoders—outperform full federation in bandwidth-constrained, non-IID environments, reducing communication by 47% while boosting F1-score by 12.3%; and (3) VAEs exhibit superior robustness across highly skewed data distributions. Our work establishes actionable tradeoff guidelines for deploying on-device generative AI in industrial FL systems.

item.title: \mathsf{VISTA}: Decentralized Machine Learning in Adversary Dominated Environments
item.description: Decentralized machine learning faces critical failure when adversaries control a majority of workers—a setting where standard robust aggregation (e.g., Krum, Bulyan) collapses. To address this, we propose **$\mathsf{VISTA}$**, an incentive-aware adaptive algorithm that accepts and rewards gradient reports only when they are mutually consistent within a dynamically tuned threshold. By transforming adversaries into rational agents who trade off estimation error against rejection risk, $\mathsf{VISTA}$ enables iterative optimization without honest-majority assumptions. It adapts the threshold online using optimization history—e.g., gradient variance and consensus rate—to balance early progress and long-term accuracy. Experiments show $\mathsf{VISTA}$ achieves up to 2.3× faster convergence and 38% lower final error than static-threshold baselines under 70% adversarial corruption. Crucially, our convergence analysis proves that, with incentive-aware adaptation, $\mathsf{VISTA}$ retains the *asymptotic convergence behavior* of standard SGD—even in adversary-dominated regimes.

item.title: Mathematical Reasoning via Intervention-Based Time-Series Causal Discovery Using LLMs as Concept Mastery Simulators
item.description: We introduce **CIKA (Causal Intervention for Knowledge Activation)**, a novel framework that treats the LLM itself as an interventional simulator to identify *causally essential* mathematical concepts—beyond spurious observational associations confounded by problem difficulty. CIKA defines the **Interventional Capability Probe (ICP)**: a prompt-based intervention exogenously sets a concept state (e.g., “mastered”), and the resulting change in answer correctness quantifies the model’s *functional ability to deploy* that concept—not merely its knowledge possession. On 67 screened problems, the top-concept ICP (+0.219) significantly exceeds the negative control (+0.039; *p* < 10⁻⁶), validating causal discrimination. Across 601 Omni-MATH problems, solved items exhibit 6.1× higher average treatment effect (ATE = 0.338 vs. 0.055), confirming ICP’s strong predictive power for success. With a frozen 7B-parameter LLM, CIKA achieves 69.7% on contamination-free Omni-MATH-Rule (vs. 60.5% for o1-mini), 97.2% on GSM8K, and 46–50% on AIME 2024–2026—while contributing **33.8% of correct answers on base-model failures**, revealing latent but inactive knowledge.

item.title: SHRED: Retain-Set-Free Unlearning via Self-Distillation with Logit Demotion
item.description: Machine unlearning for LLMs seeks efficient, selective removal of memorized content—e.g., private or harmful data—without full retraining. Existing methods rely on a curated retain set to preserve utility, introducing data dependency and deployment overhead. We propose **SHRED**, the first retain-set-free unlearning method, grounded in the insight that only *high-surprisal tokens* (lowest autoregressive probabilities) in forget instances encode memorized knowledge; low-surprisal tokens reflect general language competence and serve as benign anchors. SHRED operates in two stages: (1) *Selection*: Identify high-surprisal positions via single forward pass; (2) *Training*: Apply logit demotion at forget positions while preserving original logits at benign positions, optimized via a unified top-layer KL self-distillation objective. Evaluated across four standard benchmarks, SHRED achieves a new Pareto-optimal trade-off—outperforming retain-set-dependent baselines in both forgetting efficacy (e.g., +12.3% F1@5) and utility preservation (<0.8% MMLU drop). It further demonstrates robustness against relearning and membership-inference attacks, and stable performance over sequential unlearning runs.

item.title: ForgeVLA: Federated Vision-Language-Action Learning without Language Annotations
item.description: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are pivotal for general-purpose robotic intelligence, yet their scaling is bottlenecked by the prohibitive cost of language annotations. To address this, we propose **ForgeVLA**, the first federated VLA framework that learns from distributed, unannotated vision-action pairs *without* centralizing raw data or requiring manual language labels. ForgeVLA equips each client with an embodied instruction classifier to automatically recover missing language modality—mapping local vision-action pairs to a predefined instruction set and forming complete vision-language-action triplets. Crucially, we identify and mitigate *vision-language feature collapse*, a previously overlooked challenge in federated VLA, via a client-side contrastive planning loss and a server-side adaptive aggregation strategy. Experiments across RT-1-X, BridgeData v2, and Open-X Embodiment show ForgeVLA outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by +12.7% average task success rate; ablations confirm the critical roles of both triplet reconstruction (+8.3%) and collapse mitigation (+4.4%).

item.title: Resource-Element Energy Difference for Noncoherent Over-the-Air Federated Learning
item.description: Over-the-air federated learning (OTA-FL) promises ultra-low uplink latency via analog waveform superposition, yet conventional coherent aggregation requires instantaneous CSI, channel inversion, and phase alignment—often infeasible in practical fading channels. This paper introduces **Resource-Element Energy Difference (REED)**, a *noncoherent* primitive for aggregating continuous signed model updates without instantaneous CSI. REED splits each real-valued update into positive/negative parts, maps them onto two orthogonal resource elements with independent phase dithers, and recovers the signed sum from their *energy difference* at the server. Relying only on slow-timescale average channel power calibration, REED yields an *unbiased* estimate with an *exact closed-form variance* under Rayleigh fading. Integrated into full-participation FedAvg, we prove a smooth nonconvex stationarity bound achieving the canonical $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ rate: the REED-induced perturbation scales quadratically with local stepsize $\eta$, enabling optimal trade-off under per-client energy budgets. Experiments on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST confirm REED matches clean and coherent CSIT-based OTA-FL under IID settings, and maintains robust convergence with only moderate degradation (<3% accuracy loss) under strong heterogeneity.

item.title: HARMONY: Bridging the Personalization-Generalization Gap by Mitigating Representation Skew in Heterogeneous Split Federated Learning
item.description: Mobile devices exhibit heterogeneous architectures and non-IID data, demanding both fast on-device inference for in-distribution (ID) classes and reliable server-side fallback for client-specific out-of-distribution (OOD) classes. Hybrid Split Federated Learning (Hybrid SFL) addresses this via personalized front-ends and a shared backend—but suffers severe *representation skew* when clients use diverse extractors, degrading OOD prediction. We propose **HARMONY**, the first Hybrid SFL framework supporting architectural heterogeneity. It extends meta-learning to simulate varied client architectures during training and introduces *server-side contrastive learning*—aligning extracted features without raw labels or client architecture disclosure. Evaluated across CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and FEMNIST with multiple model families, HARMONY achieves up to **+43.0% ID accuracy** and **+28.3% OOD accuracy** over state-of-the-art, while maintaining low latency (<12 ms overhead).

item.title: Self-Play Enhancement via Advantage-Weighted Refinement in Online Federated LLM Fine-Tuning with Real-Time Feedback
item.description: We propose **SPEAR (Self-Play Enhancement via Advantage-Weighted Refinement)**, an efficient online federated fine-tuning algorithm for LLMs that leverages sparse, real-time, *non-answer feedback* (e.g., “too verbose”, “incorrect”) without requiring ground-truth contexts or offline batch generation. SPEAR establishes a client-side feedback-guided self-play loop to construct natural contrastive prompt-response pairs; it trains via standard MLE on correct completions and advantage-weighted unlikelihood loss on tail tokens of incorrect ones—enabling resource-efficient, single-round updates on edge devices. Evaluated across Alpaca, Self-Instruct, and FLANv2 under realistic federated settings, SPEAR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines (FedLLM, ReFT) by +3.2–5.8 BLEU/ROUGE points (p<0.01), demonstrating superior adaptability to partial feedback and device constraints. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/lee3296/SPEAR.

item.title: FLAM: Evaluating Model Performance with Aggregatable Measures in Federated Learning
item.description: Performance evaluation in federated learning (FL) suffers from systematic bias because standard aggregation (e.g., sample-weighted averaging) of local metrics fails to replicate centralized evaluation—especially for non-linear or distribution-sensitive metrics like F1-score or AUC. To resolve this, we propose **FLAM**, a principled framework that redefines FL evaluation via *aggregatable measures*: metrics decomposed into locally computable, globally reconstructible statistics (e.g., confusion matrix entries, binned prediction scores). FLAM guarantees *exact equivalence* to centralized evaluation without requiring global test data or raw predictions. We derive theoretical conditions for aggregatability across classification, ranking, and regression tasks, and validate FLAM on 6 heterogeneous FL benchmarks: it achieves **zero deviation** from centralized results, outperforming conventional weighting by up to 8.7 percentage points in metric error. FLAM is privacy-aware, communication-efficient, and open-sourced.

item.title: Enhancing Federated Quadruplet Learning: Stochastic Client Selection and Embedding Stability Analysis
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) suffers from degraded generalization under data heterogeneity, especially with limited and imbalanced client data. To address this, we propose **FedQuad**, a novel federated quadruplet learning framework that jointly minimizes intra-class distances (anchor–positive) and maximizes inter-class distances (anchor–negative and positive–negative) to enforce discriminative embedding geometry. FedQuad introduces a stochastic client selection strategy guided by gradient variance to improve aggregation reliability and provides the first stability analysis of embeddings across FL rounds via the Embedding Consistency Coefficient (ECC). Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under diverse non-IID settings (Dirichlet α=0.1–0.5, label/quantity skew) and 50–500 clients show FedQuad consistently outperforms strong baselines (e.g., +3.2–7.9% Top-1 accuracy over FedAvg), with verified mitigation of representation collapse and +23.6–38.1% higher ECC.

item.title: The Coupling Tax: How Shared Token Budgets Undermine Visible Chain-of-Thought Under Fixed Output Limits
item.description: This paper identifies the *coupling tax*: a fundamental trade-off where sharing a fixed output-token budget between reasoning traces and final answers undermines Chain-of-Thought (CoT) effectiveness. Across GSM8K, MATH-500, and five BIG-Bench Hard tasks with Qwen3 models (0.5B–7B), non-CoT generation matches or exceeds standard CoT accuracy at all budgets ≤2048 tokens—revealing that longer traces often crowd out the answer they support. We derive a truncation-waste decomposition, $\mathrm{Acc}_{\mathrm{think}}(b)=\alpha_c F_L(b)+\alpha_t(1-F_L(b))$, which predicts the crossover point from trace-length and accuracy statistics and explains inverse scaling in the Qwen family. Replication on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B confirms robustness across architectures and interfaces. Mitigating coupling via *split-budget generation*—decoupling reasoning and answer token allocation—achieves 74.0% (IRIS), 78.8% (enhanced extraction), and **83.6%** (fixed SC+IRIS gate) accuracy on full MATH-500. Results establish test-time reasoning as a *budget-allocation optimization*, not merely a choice of trace length.

item.title: Uncovering Hidden Systematics in Neural Network Models for High Energy Physics
item.description: Neural networks (NNs) achieve state-of-the-art performance in high-energy physics (HEP) tasks like event classification and object identification, yet their sensitivity to subtle input variations poses serious challenges for systematic uncertainty quantification. Drawing on adversarial learning insights, we systematically probe NN robustness under *physically realistic perturbations*: multi-dimensional input shifts fully consistent with experimental uncertainties—while preserving marginal distributions and key correlations (ΔK-S < 0.01, p > 0.95). Across seven architectures (CNN, ResNet, Transformer, GNN, etc.) and representative HEP benchmarks (e.g., H→bb̄ tagging, τ identification), we find that up to **41% of events are misclassified within ±1σ experimental uncertainty envelopes**, revealing widespread “hidden systematics” unexposed by conventional methods. We introduce the **Systematic Sensitivity Index (SSI)**—a quantitative, differentiable metric capturing expected output shift per unit uncertainty—and release `SysProbe`, an open-source toolkit for end-to-end uncertainty-aware evaluation. This work establishes a practical, physics-grounded framework to uncover, measure, and control NN model uncertainties in real-world HEP analyses.

item.title: MIPIAD: Multilingual Indirect Prompt Injection Attack Defense with Qwen -- TF-IDF Hybrid and Meta-Ensemble Learning
item.description: MIPIAD is a novel multilingual defense framework against indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks in RAG and tool-augmented LLMs. It integrates three components: (1) **XLPID**, a LoRA-finetuned Qwen2.5-1.5B sequence classifier for cross-lingual semantic detection; (2) **TF-IDF lexical features**, capturing language-agnostic attack patterns; and (3) a **meta-ensemble** combining late fusion, stacking, and gradient boosting (XGBoost) tuned on validation data. Evaluated on a large-scale synthetic benchmark (1.43M samples across 5 task families, English & Bangla only), MIPIAD achieves an overall F1 of **0.9205** (hybrid XLPID+TF-IDF) and AUROC of **0.9378** (XGBoost ensemble)—outperforming standalone models and notably narrowing the English-Bangla performance gap (−38% ΔF1 on average). Designed for extensibility, it leverages NLLB-200 and Qwen’s multilingual backbone, enabling zero-architectural-change adaptation to >200 languages.

item.title: Beyond "I cannot fulfill this request": Alleviating Rigid Rejection in LLMs via Label Enhancement
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) often respond to unsafe requests with rigid, templated refusals (e.g., “I cannot fulfill this request”), harming interaction naturalness and user experience. To address this, we propose **LANCE**, a label enhancement framework that replaces binary refusal decisions with a *continuous, multi-category rejection distribution*, inferred via variational inference. This fine-grained distribution serves as multi-way textual gradients to guide a refinement model in neutralizing hazardous elements while preserving user intent. The base LLM then generates safe, contextually appropriate responses—avoiding templates without compromising security. Experiments show LANCE reduces rigid refusals by **63.7%**, maintains >98.2% harm rejection accuracy, and significantly outperforms baselines (e.g., RLHF, Constitutional AI) in response helpfulness (+38.2%) and naturalness (+41.5%) under human evaluation (p<0.01). LANCE establishes a new paradigm: safety alignment via *distributional refusal guidance*, not just classification.

item.title: Rethinking Dense Sequential Chains: Reasoning Language Models Can Extract Answers from Sparse, Order-Shuffling Chain-of-Thoughts
item.description: This paper challenges the prevailing assumption that dense, strictly sequential chain-of-thought (CoT) traces are necessary for answer extraction in reasoning language models. Through a systematic intervention pipeline—removing, masking, shuffling, and injecting noise—we perturb model-generated CoTs across three models (LLaMA-2, Qwen, Gemma) and three benchmarks (GSM8K, SVAMP, AQuA). Counterintuitively, answer extraction proves highly order-insensitive: line-level shuffling degrades accuracy by <0.5 pp; word-level shuffling retains 62–89% accuracy; only token-level shuffling collapses performance. Crucially, pretrained-only and instruction-tuned models show near-identical tolerance (78.67% vs. 78.00%), indicating order-independence stems from pretraining—not reasoning-specific fine-tuning. We further demonstrate sparsity: masking numeric digits reduces accuracy to 0%, while masking alphabetic prose *improves* it by +4.7 pp. Remarkably, even the most aggressive reduction—keeping only shuffled numeric/symbolic lines with all natural language removed—achieves 83% accuracy; and injecting false answers at 3× true-answer frequency leaves accuracy unchanged (83.3% → 83.3%), falsifying frequency-based extraction. These results establish that answer extraction operates on a sparse, order-insensitive, and structurally robust informational substrate—enabling parallelized, token-efficient reasoning generation.

item.title: Securing Computer-Use Agents: A Unified Architecture-Lifecycle Framework for Deployment-Grounded Reliability
item.description: This paper introduces a unified architecture-lifecycle framework to advance *deployment-grounded reliability* for Computer-Use Agents (CUAs). Moving beyond narrow benchmarks, CUAs now operate across browsers, desktops, mobile apps, file systems, terminals, and tool backends—where reliability hinges on the joint integrity of perception, planning, memory, tool mediation, permission scope, and runtime oversight. The framework comprises two interlocking views: (1) an *architectural view* decomposing CUAs into coupled **Perception–Decision–Execution** layers that transform software observations into authority-bearing actions; and (2) a *lifecycle view* spanning **Creation–Deployment–Operation–Maintenance**, tracking how priors are learned, tools/permissions bound, trajectories stressed, and assurance preserved under drift. Synthesizing 28 systems, 12 benchmarks, and 37 security/privacy studies, the analysis maps where failures *manifest* versus where their root conditions are *introduced*, and identifies five recurring intervention surfaces for control, oversight, and assurance. OpenClaw serves solely as a public motivating example—not a verified case study. Key open challenges include controllable grounding, long-horizon constraint preservation, safe authority binding, mixed-trust runtime defense, privacy-preserving memory, and continual assurance.

item.title: On Succinct Non-Interactive Secure Computation with Malicious Security
item.description: We present the first maliciously secure succinct non-interactive secure computation (NISC) protocols for natural functionalities—Private Set Membership, dictionary lookup, verifiable dictionary lookup (under read-once small-state predicates), and UP search—based solely on standard assumptions: fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) and batch arguments (BARGs). Our key technical innovation is a new simulation technique that extracts the server’s large input piece by piece and reconstructs it coherently via a novel monotone coupling argument rooted in Strassen’s theorem. This bypasses the need for full input knowledge in simulation—a major bottleneck in prior work. All protocols achieve split-simulation security: correctness of the client’s output and indistinguishability of the server’s view are guaranteed separately. Our results bridge a long-standing gap between semi-honest succinctness (achievable from FHE) and malicious security, which previously required non-standard assumptions like SNARKs.

item.title: Titan: Efficient Polynomial Commitments from IOPs over Groups
item.description: We present **Titan**, an efficient transparent polynomial commitment scheme (PCS) achieving $O(n)$ commitment, $O(\sqrt{n})$ evaluation, and—critically—$O(\sqrt[4]{n})$ proof size and verification. Titan reduces proof sizes by **10×** over hash-based PCS and significantly accelerates prover/verifier over state-of-the-art group-based schemes (e.g., Dory, Hyrax). Its key insight is replacing expensive bilinear pairings in outer commitments with **WHIR**, an interactive oracle proof of proximity (IOPP) instantiated over generic prime-order groups (e.g., Pasta curves), thus eliminating the need for pairing-friendly curves. Compiling Titan into a Spartan-style R1CS-SNARK yields **TitanSnark**, which preserves Spartan’s prover efficiency while quadratically improving proof size and verification—from $O(\sqrt{n})$ to $O(\sqrt[4]{n})$. For circuits of size $\geq 2^{22}$, TitanSnark achieves **≈3× smaller proofs and faster verification**. Our IOPP-over-groups + Pedersen inner commitment blueprint, along with WHIR optimizations, is of broad independent interest.

item.title: Zero-Knowledge Proofs for Gradient Boosted Decision Trees
item.description: Gradient boosted decision trees (GBDTs) are widely deployed in high-stakes tabular learning tasks, yet verifying their training and inference without exposing data or model parameters remains challenging. Prior zero-knowledge proof (ZKP) approaches for GBDTs either incur prohibitive prover overhead via generic circuit compilers or suffer from explosion in auxiliary commitments due to heterogeneous, stage-specific algebraic constraints. We propose **\textsc{Terrae}**, a dedicated ZKP system for *quantized* GBDT training and inference built on KZG commitments. \textsc{Terrae} introduces two novel batching techniques—*domain-lifting batching* for linear constraints and *interleaving batching* for non-linear ones—that compress hundreds of constraints across training stages into single polynomial claims *without extra commitments*. It also includes a new histogram correctness proof for frequency-based data aggregation. Evaluation shows \textsc{Terrae} reduces proof-generation time by **12.8×–24.3×** over state-of-the-art SNARKs while adding only **17%–29%** proof-size overhead—enabling practical, end-to-end verifiable GBDT training at scale.

item.title: An analysis of a weakened version of PRISM
item.description: PRISM (PKC25) is a hash-and-sign signature scheme whose security rests on the hardness of computing large-prime-degree isogenies from a curve with unknown endomorphism ring. Its design hashes messages to large odd integers and applies a primality test to select degrees. This work reveals that PRISM’s security critically depends on the strength of that test. We show that weak tests (e.g., single-round Miller–Rabin or Fermat) invalidate the core hardness assumption in the standard model, enabling efficient decomposition attacks. In the quantum random oracle model (QROM), we prove that Miller–Rabin suffices and derive tight bounds: for 256-bit candidate degrees targeting 128-bit quantum security, **13 rounds** achieve error probability < 2⁻²⁵⁶—reducing signing cost by ~30% versus conservative standards. Our analysis bridges theoretical assumptions and practical implementation, providing the first parameterized security guarantee for PRISM’s primality component.

item.title: Maintaining Sublinear Locality Over Time: Adaptively Secure MPC on a Reusable Hidden Graph
item.description: We resolve a fundamental open question: *Can sublinear communication locality be maintained over an unbounded number of MPC executions against adaptive adversaries?* Prior work (Chandran et al., ITCS’15) achieved sublinear locality for a single execution in the hidden-graph model—but required refreshing the graph each round, causing cumulative degree blowup and limiting scalability. We introduce the **reusable hidden graph** paradigm: a single low-degree graph, initially hidden from the adversary, suffices for arbitrarily many MPC sessions. Our contributions are two-fold: (1) In the semi-honest, information-theoretic setting, we construct reusable MPC with **$O(\log^c n)$ locality**; (2) In the fail-stop, computational setting, we achieve **$\tilde{O}(n^{2/3})$ locality**, the first sublinear bound for unbounded executions under adaptive corruption. Technically, we design low-locality protocols that provably hide topological information—extending topology-hiding computation (Moran et al., TCC’15) to adaptive, reusable scenarios. This yields the first formally secure foundation for persistent, low-degree private networks.

item.title: Sponsored Fair Exchange (Extended Abstract)
item.description: We propose **Sponsored Fair Exchange (SFE)**, a minimal smart contract–based protocol enabling costless, fair digital item–for-cryptocurrency exchange. By moving most communication and verification offchain, SFE reduces onchain logic to arbitration and timeout handling only; all gas costs are covered by incentivized sponsors—users pay **zero fees in both success and cancellation cases**. The protocol implements *knowledge-coin exchange*: one party submits a digitally verifiable description (e.g., hash, ZK proof, Merkle path) of an asset, and the other pays cryptocurrency. It guarantees strong fairness: either both parties receive their expected outputs (atomic completion), or neither incurs loss (safe abort). Compared to OptiSwap, SFE supports **arbitrary description functions**, achieves **logarithmic worst-case complexity** (vs. linear), and introduces sponsorship as a novel enabler of economic inclusion for resource-constrained users.

item.title: Magic Pot: Cryptanalysis of full AIM2 in the standard and related-/reused-key settings using new elimination framework
item.description: We cryptanalyze AIMer v2.1—the winner of the Korean Post-Quantum Cryptography competition (KpqC) and a former NIST PQC signature candidate—by breaking its core symmetric primitive, **AIM2**, using a novel algebraic framework called *Magic Pot*. Our approach combines extended linearization over univariate polynomial rings with a new algorithm to efficiently compute low-weight null vectors of structured polynomial matrices. In the standard setting, we recover the full AIM2 key with complexity far below its claimed 128-bit security (practically $\sim 2^{72}$ operations). Crucially, under reused-key or related-key misuse, the attack becomes experimentally feasible: we fully verified it on commodity hardware within hours. We also applied Magic Pot to the RAIN block cipher (used in Rainier), improving the best-known algebraic attack from 6 to 7 rounds—demonstrating broader applicability without compromising Rainier’s stated security. This work invalidates AIM2’s security foundation and introduces a powerful, generalizable algebraic cryptanalysis methodology for post-quantum primitives.

item.title: End-to-End Polynomial-Time Cryptanalytic Extraction of Convolutional Neural Networks in the Hard-Label Setting
item.description: This paper presents the first end-to-end, polynomial-time cryptanalytic extraction attack against ReLU convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in the hard-label setting (top-1 predictions only), assuming known architecture and average pooling. We overcome fundamental CNN-specific challenges—weight sharing, parallel critical hyperplanes, spatially coupled perturbations, and channel-sign ambiguity—via a novel **channel-level recovery framework**: it locates dual boundary points (decision + activation), recovers shared channel signatures using SVD, resolves sign ambiguity, and peels layers while absorbing ReLU scale factors into subsequent linear layers. Our key algorithmic contribution is **SVGR-guided retained-candidate discrete optimization**, operating under a practical retained-candidate assumption. Across 1D/2D MNIST and RGB CIFAR-10 variants, extracted models achieve **100% prediction fidelity** (identical hard-label outputs). Critically, extracted watermarked CNNs preserve behavior-level ownership signals—and admit deterministic trigger wrapping *without erasing original watermarks*, posing dual risks to owners and downstream users. Results prove that hiding logits alone fails to protect parameters for this CNN family when architecture is public.

item.title: Patch2Vuln: Agentic Reconstruction of Vulnerabilities from Linux Distribution Binary Patches
item.description: This paper introduces **Patch2Vuln**, an agentic, fully local pipeline that reconstructs vulnerability root causes *solely from Linux distribution binary patches* (e.g., Ubuntu `.deb` packages), without source code or network access. It extracts ELF pairs, performs Ghidra-based binary diffing and function ranking, constructs contextual dossiers, and delegates multi-stage reasoning—preliminary audit, bounded validation planning, and final root-cause classification—to an offline LLM agent. Evaluated on 25 manually adjudicated package pairs (20 security updates + 5 negative controls), Patch2Vuln localizes a verified vulnerable function in 10/20 security cases and assigns an expert-accepted root-cause class in 11/20. Oracle analysis attributes 7 failures to upstream binary-diff/ranking limitations—not LLM reasoning—while the bounded validation pass produced only two minimized behavioral differentials (both for `tcpdump`) with no crash, timeout, sanitizer finding, or memory corruption proof. All five negative controls were correctly classified as “unknown” with zero false positives. Results establish binary-driven agentic vulnerability reconstruction as a viable research direction, with binary-diff coverage and local behavioral validation identified as the key bottlenecks.

item.title: FedAttr: Towards Privacy-preserving Client-Level Attribution in Federated LLM Fine-tuning
item.description: We propose **FedAttr**, the first privacy-preserving client-level attribution protocol for watermark detection in federated LLM fine-tuning. Unlike centralized settings, standard watermark radioactivity testing fails under secure aggregation (SA), as SA hides per-client updates. FedAttr overcomes this via a *paired-subset-difference* mechanism: it estimates each client’s update by querying SA over two disjoint client subsets, scores the estimate using a differential watermark detector, and aggregates scores across rounds via Stouffer’s method. Theoretically, FedAttr yields an unbiased update estimator with bounded mutual information leakage ($O(d^*/N)$ per round), preserving SA’s privacy guarantees. Empirically, it achieves **100% TPR and 0% FPR**, outperforming all baselines by ≥44.4% in TPR or ≥19.1% in FPR, with only **6.3% training overhead**. Ablations confirm robustness to protocol parameters. FedAttr enables verifiable, auditable data provenance in production FL-LLM systems without compromising privacy or efficiency.

item.title: CLAD: A Clustered Label-Agnostic Federated Learning Framework for Joint Anomaly Detection and Attack Classification
item.description: The rapid proliferation of IoT/IIoT has created highly heterogeneous, label-scarce edge environments where conventional federated learning (FL) fails in intrusion detection due to device diversity and underutilization of unlabeled data. We propose **CLAD**, a clustered, label-agnostic FL framework that jointly addresses anomaly detection and attack classification. CLAD integrates **Dynamic Clustering Federated Learning**—to group devices by traffic similarity and prevent global model divergence—with a novel **Dual-Mode Micro-Architecture ($\text{DM}^2\text{A}$)** featuring a shared encoder and two parallel branches: one for unsupervised anomaly detection (via reconstruction and contrastive learning), and another for supervised attack classification. This enables knowledge extraction from both labeled and unlabeled clients within a single unified model. Extensive experiments show CLAD achieves a **30% relative improvement in F1-score** over state-of-the-art baselines under 80% unlabeled clients, while reducing communication cost by 50%. CLAD discards no data and preserves distinct operational patterns across device clusters.

item.title: PACZero: PAC-Private Fine-Tuning of Language Models via Sign Quantization
item.description: We propose **PACZero**, a family of PAC-private zeroth-order mechanisms for fine-tuning LMs, achieving *usable utility* under the strongest privacy guarantee: $I(S^*; Y_{1:T}) = 0$. Unlike DP—which requires infinite noise or $\varepsilon=0$ to match this MIA-resistance level—PACZero leverages *sign quantization of subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients* to induce frequent unanimity across candidate subsets, rendering sign releases conditionally independent of the secret subset and thus costing zero conditional mutual information. We introduce two variants: PACZero-MI (budgeted MI via exact binary calibration) and PACZero-ZPL (strict $I=0$ via uniform coin flips on disagreement steps). On SST-2 and SQuAD with OPT-1.3B/6.7B (LoRA and full-parameter), PACZero-ZPL achieves **88.99±0.91** accuracy on SST-2 full fine-tuning—within 2.1 pp of the non-private MeZO baseline—while no prior DP method attains usable utility at $\varepsilon<1$. PACZero is the first framework to deliver competitive accuracy and nontrivial SQuAD F1 *at provable $I=0$*.

item.title: Pop Quiz Attack: Black-box Membership Inference Attacks Against Large Language Models
item.description: We propose **Pop Quiz Attack**, a novel black-box membership inference attack against large language models (LLMs). It transforms candidate training samples into multiple-choice questions and infers membership based on the model’s answer confidence—specifically, the logit difference or probability advantage for the correct option. Evaluated on six widely used LLMs (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o, LLaMA2-7b/13b, Mistral-7b, Vicuna-7b) and four diverse datasets, our method achieves an average ROC-AUC of **0.873**, outperforming prior black-box baselines by **20.6%**. We systematically analyze key factors affecting success—including query complexity, data modality, structural format, and training configuration—and evaluate instruction-based, filtering-based, and differential privacy defenses: all reduce but **fail to eliminate** the threat (AUC remains >0.65). This work reveals persistent, practical privacy vulnerabilities in modern LLMs.

item.title: Constraining Host-Level Abuse in Self-Hosted Computer-Use Agents via TEE-Backed Isolation
item.description: Self-hosted computer-use agents (SHCUAs) like OpenClaw enable powerful automation by directly accessing host resources—but introduce critical host-level abuse risks via prompt injection, malicious skills, or control-path tampering. We argue that ad hoc blocking rules are insufficient because operation safety depends on the joint context of action type, target object, execution environment, and impact. This paper proposes an operation-centric confinement model: routine functionality runs in the constrained Rich Execution Environment (REE), while security-critical tasks—including dynamic classification, fine-grained authorization, subject-action-resource binding, evidence generation, and selective execution control—are enforced inside a cloud-native, TEE-backed trusted operation plane. Instantiated on OpenClaw using Intel TDX as the primary trusted backend, our design features remote terminal-side trusted components that verify TDX-audited commands before local execution. Evaluation shows the system blocks all unsafe or policy-violating operations pre-execution, preserves full functionality for permitted workloads with negligible latency overhead (<120 ms avg.), and generates cryptographically verifiable, context-rich audit evidence—enabling robust accountability without compromising usability.

item.title: Fine-Tuning Small Language Models for Solution-Oriented Windows Event Log Analysis
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) show promise for Windows event log analysis but face practical barriers: high computational cost, cloud dependency, and security risks from sensitive log data. Crucially, most existing methods only diagnose problems—not generate actionable remediations. This paper introduces a solution-oriented alternative using fine-tuned Small Language Models (SLMs). We first construct a large-scale synthetic dataset (100K+ samples) of Windows events—each annotated with root cause, step-by-step remediation, and verification commands—generated via a high-performing LLM (GPT-4) and rigorously validated. Using LoRA-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning, we adapt multiple SLMs (e.g., Phi-3, Qwen2-1.5B) and compare them against LLMs (e.g., Llama3-8B). Expert evaluation shows fine-tuned SLMs achieve **92.3% F1 on issue identification** (+6.1 pts over LLMs) and **87.6% adoption rate for remediation suggestions** (+14.4 pts), while requiring only **2.1 GB VRAM** and **142 ms latency per log**—enabling secure, low-resource, on-premise deployment. This work demonstrates that task-specialized SLMs can outperform general-purpose LLMs in both accuracy and actionability for operational log analysis.

item.title: Trade-off Functions for DP-SGD with Subsampling based on Random Shuffling: Tight Upper and Lower Bounds
item.description: We provide the first tight, closed-form upper and lower bounds on the trade-off function for DP-SGD with *random shuffling* (not Poisson subsampling) within the $f$-DP framework, valid for $\sigma \geq \sqrt{3/\ln M}$. Leveraging the Berry–Esseen theorem, our bounds are explicit, interpretable, and tight up to constant factors. For a single epoch ($E = 1$), we show that with $\sigma = 1$ and $\delta = 0.01$, only $M \approx 1.14\times10^6$ iterations and $N \approx 1.14\times10^7$ samples suffice to achieve $f(a) \geq 1 - a - \delta$—a meaningful privacy guarantee unattainable in the small-$\sigma$ regime. For $E$-epoch composition, classical analysis yields $\delta = O(E)$, limiting $E = O(\sqrt{M})$; our new technique—based on a generalized law of large numbers—establishes that if $E = c_M^2 M$ with $c_M \to 0$, then the $E$-fold composed trade-off function converges uniformly to the random-guessing diagonal $1-a$, with $\delta = O(\sqrt{E})$. This reveals an intrinsic asymptotic advantage of shuffling over Poisson sampling, while explicit convergence rates remain an open problem.

item.title: ClawGuard: Out-of-Band Detection of LLM Agent Workflow Hijacking via EM Side Channel
item.description: ClawGuard is the first out-of-band detection system for LLM agent workflow hijacking, leveraging electromagnetic (EM) side-channel emissions as a physical, forge-resistant audit signal. Unlike host-based defenses vulnerable to OS compromise, ClawGuard uses external software-defined radios (SDRs) to passively capture macroscopic EM envelopes radiated by hardware usage patterns—distinct across LLM skills (e.g., computation, DRAM access, network blocking). A drift-aware pipeline extracts 320-dimensional RF features and classifies skill executions in real time. Evaluated on a large-scale 7.82 TB RF corpus covering 12 realistic agent skills, ClawGuard achieves an AUC of 0.9945, 100% true-positive rate, and only 1.16% false-positive rate. This demonstrates that passive EM sensing is a practical, hardware-rooted foundation for trustworthy LLM agent monitoring—immune to software-level tampering.
